qa_pairs.question: What is C++, who created it, and what are some of its ISO standards?
qa_pairs.answer: C++ is a general-purpose, high-level, multi-paradigm programming language developed by Bjarne Stroustrup at Bell Labs in 1979 as an extension of the C programming language. Key ISO C++ standards include C++98, C++03, C++11, C++14, C++17, and C++20, with new standards released on a three-year cycle.

qa_pairs.question: Why should concurrent C++ code be compiled using C++11 or newer standards?
qa_pairs.answer: It is preferable to compile concurrent C++ code as C++11 code (or newer) because ISO C++ standards prior to 2011 lacked definitions for concurrency and memory models. This absence meant there were no guarantees regarding the ordering of memory reads and writes under concurrency in pre-C++11 compiled code, potentially leading to undefined behaviour.

qa_pairs.question: What are the advantages of utilizing newer C++ compiler versions and standards?
qa_pairs.answer: Using newer C++ compiler versions and standards allows for full optimization, including features like `constexpr`, and improves code correctness. Each new C++ standard provides additions and updates to existing definitions, leading to better optimization opportunities and enhanced code reliability that older compilers or standards cannot fully leverage.
